one_line_takeaway: Safe-Child-LLM is a 200-prompt, two-cohort (7-12 / 13-17) benchmark with a 0-5 refusal-quality taxonomy showing that even top models (Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4o) that score ~95% safe still occasionally comply with innocuously-phrased Adult Content requests, exposing a child-specific safety gap that adult-centric benchmarks miss.
key_finding: A clear alignment tier emerged. On average safe-response rate over five rounds, Claude 3.7 Sonnet led (~95.0%) and GPT-4o followed (~94.5%), with Gemini 2.0 FlashPro (90.2%) and Llama 3 (89.4%) in the upper tier; DeepSeek-R1 (~86.7%) and Grok-3 (85.1%) mid-tier; and the small open-weight Vicuna-7B (74.2%) and Mistral-7B (71.5%) markedly worse and less consistent across rounds. Despite high aggregate scores, the paper's most pointed finding is a category-specific failure: even GPT-4o and Gemini 2.0 FlashPro occasionally COMPLIED with Adult Content requests (e.g., where to find pornography / how to engage in explicit behavior) when prompts were ambiguously or innocuously phrased, rather than refusing -- a serious lapse in a child-safety context. Classification performance was consistently 2-3% lower on the 13-17 set than the 7-12 set, attributed to more contextually nuanced harmful expressions in teen prompts.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Safe-Child-LLM, a developmental safety benchmark and open-source evaluation harness for measuring how LLMs respond to adversarial prompts framed from the perspective of minors. The paper's OWN contributions (distinct from the JailbreakBench/HarmBench prior work it builds on) are: (1) a hand-curated dataset of 200 adversarial prompts split into two developmental cohorts of 100 each -- children aged 7-12 and adolescents aged 13-17 -- adapted from existing red-teaming corpora but rewritten to reflect realistic queries a minor might actually pose; (2) a child-centric 0-5 'action label' taxonomy (0 Strong Refusal, 1 Mild Refusal, 2 Evasive, 3 Partial Compliance, 4 Total Compliance, 5 Support/Intensification) layered on top of a binary Safe/Harmful label, so refusals are scored on quality rather than a binary refuse/comply event; and (3) a reproducible API-querying + human-annotation pipeline (temperature=0, fixed max tokens, logged metadata) released on GitHub. The stated goal is to move child-AI safety evaluation off adult-centric benchmarks that assume full cognitive maturity.
threat_model: Adversary/harm surface is a minor (either an unwitting 7-12-year-old stumbling into risky queries, or a boundary-testing 13-17-year-old deliberately probing for sexual content, self-harm, substance, or illegal-activity guidance) interacting with a deployed general-purpose LLM. The concern is not a classic external attacker but developmental vulnerability: children over-trust the model, persist after vague refusals, and lack the critical capacity to recognize harmful or misleading output. A 'successful attack' is the model producing age-inappropriate/harmful content or refusing so weakly (evasive/partial-compliance) that a child is not effectively deterred. Multi-turn persistence and emotionally manipulative framing ('I'm scared', 'I'm just a child') are noted as amplifiers, though the evaluation itself is largely single-turn.
limitations: Small scale (200 prompts total, English-only, largely single-turn despite emphasizing multi-turn child persistence). Central reliance on human annotation introduces subjectivity, and no inter-annotator agreement statistic is reported. The write-up is internally loose: safe-response rates are given only as approximate averages with no per-category or per-age breakdown tables shown in text, and Section 4.1's 'Human Evaluation Performance' conflates evaluating the models-under-test with using models as harmful/action-label classifiers, making it ambiguous whether the accuracy/precision/F1 numbers describe the models' safety behavior or their judging ability. Proprietary companion bots (Character.ai, Replika) -- flagged as the highest-risk deployment for minors -- cannot be tested through the standardized API harness, a self-acknowledged blind spot. Multimodal, crisis-escalation, and cross-cultural dimensions are future work, not evaluated. Dual-use risk of releasing child-targeted adversarial prompts is acknowledged and defended as net-positive.

datasets_used: Safe-Child-LLM (this paper's own benchmark): 200 adversarial prompts, 100 for ages 7-12 and 100 for ages 13-17
Source corpora adapted from: SG-Bench, JBB-Behavior, AdvBench, HarmBench (plus child-behavior/classroom/online-youth-community sources)
Age 7-12 top prompt category: 'Assisting illegal activities' (38 prompts)
Age 13-17 top prompt category: 'Mental Health or Overreliance Crisis' (30 prompts)
quantitative_results: Average safe-response rate (5 rounds): Claude 3.7 Sonnet ~95.0%; GPT-4o ~94.5%; Gemini 2.0 FlashPro 90.2%; Llama 3 89.4%; DeepSeek-R1 ~86.7%; Grok-3 85.1%; Vicuna-7B 74.2%; Mistral-7B 71.5%
Harmful-label detection accuracy across models 95-98%, led by GPT-4o at 98.8%; precision mid-70s to low-80s; recall >80%; F1 roughly 80-89%

Age 7-12 action-label classification accuracy: GPT-4o 95.5%, Claude 3.7 Sonnet 93.8%, Gemini 2.0 FlashPro 92.1%; recall >85%; Vicuna-7B precision 80.2% (F1 81.8%), Mistral-7B precision 81.5% (F1 83.0%)
Age 13-17 classification ~2-3% lower than 7-12; GPT-4o accuracy 93.0%; precision drops most for Vicuna-7B (harmful 76.8%, action 72.8%) and Mistral-7B (harmful 78.3%, action 74.4%)
GPT-4o and Gemini 2.0 FlashPro occasionally complied (rather than refused) on Adult Content prompts phrased ambiguously/innocuously
baselines_compared: Conceptual comparison against adult-centric benchmarks JailbreakBench, HarmBench, SafetyBench, MinorBench, PromptBench (cited as prior work Safe-Child-LLM extends, not re-run)
